meta.title: DBX-DAA-2: Delta Tables, Dashboards and Data Quality

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to run complex SQL queries that join multiple large Delta tables. The analyst's manager asks them to select a SQL Warehouse size. What is the primary effect of increasing the cluster size from Small to Large on a SQL Warehouse?

options.text: The warehouse can serve more concurrent users simultaneously
Individual queries receive more CPU and memory resources, improving single-query performance
The warehouse starts up faster after the auto-stop period
The query result cache retention period increases from 24 to 48 hours

questions.explanation: B is correct because the cluster size (2X-Small through 4X-Large) controls the compute resources allocated to each cluster in the warehouse — larger sizes provide more CPU cores and memory per cluster, which improves individual query performance on large datasets. A is wrong because concurrent user capacity is increased by adding more clusters through auto-scaling, not by increasing the cluster size. C is wrong because cluster size has no effect on start-up time — serverless vs classic type and infrastructure configuration affect startup speed. D is wrong because the query result cache TTL is always 24 hours regardless of cluster size.
questions.reference: SQL Warehouse cluster sizes

questions.stem: A data analyst notices that queries against a large Delta table are scanning the full table each time, even though most queries filter by a single date. The SQL Warehouse uses Photon. Which mechanism should improve the performance of these date-filtered queries WITHOUT requiring any SQL changes?

options.text: Query result cache, because repeated queries with the same date filter will be served from cache
Disk cache (I/O cache), which caches Parquet file data in local SSD storage after the first read to speed up subsequent scans
Metadata cache, which stores the results of aggregate queries in memory
Photon engine, which rewrites the SQL query to add partition pruning automatically

questions.explanation: B is correct because the disk cache (also called the I/O cache) caches the raw Parquet file data from remote object storage (S3, ADLS, GCS) onto local SSD on the warehouse nodes. After the first full table scan, subsequent scans read from fast local SSD rather than slow remote storage, significantly reducing scan time even when different date filters are applied. A is wrong because the query result cache requires an identical query to return a cache hit — different date filter values produce different queries and will not cache-share. C is wrong because the metadata cache stores file-level statistics and metadata, not aggregate query results. D is wrong because Photon accelerates execution of SQL operations but does not rewrite queries or automatically add partition pruning based on query patterns.
questions.reference: Disk cache (I/O cache)

questions.stem: A data analyst is reviewing the Query History page in Databricks SQL and notices a query that took 8 minutes to complete. The query is marked with a status of 'Finished'. What additional information can the analyst find in Query History to diagnose why the query was slow?

options.text: The full Spark execution plan and DAG visualization for the query
The duration, rows returned, bytes scanned, and which warehouse the query ran on
The number of partitions scanned and the specific executor logs for the query
The CPU and memory utilization graphs for the warehouse during query execution

questions.explanation: B is correct because the Query History page in Databricks SQL shows key metrics for each query including total duration, number of rows returned, bytes scanned, the warehouse it ran on, the user who ran it, and the query text itself. These are the metrics available in the Databricks SQL Query History UI. A is wrong because full Spark execution plans and DAG visualizations are available in Spark UI on all-purpose clusters, not in the Databricks SQL Query History page. C is wrong because partition-level scan details and executor logs are available in Spark UI, not Query History. D is wrong because warehouse-level CPU and memory graphs are shown in the warehouse monitoring section, not per-query in Query History.
questions.reference: Query History details

questions.stem: A company has a serverless SQL Warehouse configured with a minimum of 1 cluster and a maximum of 3 clusters. Ten analysts are all submitting queries simultaneously during a peak reporting period. How does the warehouse handle this concurrency?

options.text: The warehouse queues all 10 queries and executes them sequentially on 1 cluster
The warehouse auto-scales by adding up to 2 additional clusters to handle the increased concurrency, distributing queries across up to 3 clusters
The warehouse rejects queries beyond the minimum cluster's capacity until a cluster becomes free
The warehouse creates 10 separate clusters, one per analyst, up to the maximum of 3, then queues the remaining 7

questions.explanation: B is correct because SQL Warehouses support auto-scaling: when query concurrency exceeds what the current number of clusters can handle, Databricks automatically spins up additional clusters (up to the configured maximum of 3) to distribute the workload. Multiple queries can run in parallel across the available clusters. A is wrong because with auto-scaling configured, the warehouse does not limit itself to 1 cluster — it scales out to handle concurrency. C is wrong because the warehouse does not reject queries; it queues them and scales up to the maximum cluster count. D is wrong because clusters are not dedicated per user — each cluster serves multiple queries, and the warehouse scales to a maximum of 3 total clusters regardless of how many users there are.
questions.reference: SQL Warehouse auto-scaling and concurrency

questions.stem: A data analyst saves a SQL query in Databricks SQL and wants to share it with a colleague so the colleague can view and run the query but not edit it. Which permission level should be granted on the saved query?

options.text: CAN_VIEW
CAN_RUN
CAN_EDIT
CAN_USE

questions.explanation: B is correct because CAN_RUN on a saved query allows the grantee to view the query text and execute it against the warehouse — they can run the query and see results but cannot modify the query definition. A is wrong because CAN_VIEW only allows the grantee to see the query text and cached results but does not allow them to execute the query. C is wrong because CAN_EDIT allows modifying the query SQL and settings, which exceeds the intended permission. D is wrong because CAN_USE is a SQL Warehouse permission (not a query permission) that allows using the warehouse to run queries.
questions.reference: Query permissions — CAN_RUN

questions.stem: A data analyst sets up a Databricks SQL Alert on a query that returns the count of failed transactions per hour. The alert should notify the on-call engineer when failures exceed 100. Select TWO notification destinations that are natively supported by Databricks SQL Alerts.

options.text: Email address
SMS text message
Slack webhook
Direct push notification to the Databricks mobile app

questions.explanation: A is correct because email is a natively supported notification destination for Databricks SQL Alerts — the analyst can enter one or more email addresses to receive alert notifications. C is correct because Slack webhook is a natively supported destination in Databricks SQL Alerts, allowing teams to receive alert messages directly in a Slack channel. B is wrong because SMS is not a native notification destination in Databricks SQL Alerts — SMS could only be achieved indirectly through a third-party integration like PagerDuty. D is wrong because there is no native push notification to a Databricks mobile app as a Databricks SQL Alert destination.
questions.reference: Alert notification destinations

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to understand what operations the Photon engine accelerates in Databricks SQL. Which of the following workloads will Photon NOT accelerate?

options.text: SQL SELECT queries with large table scans and filter predicates
SQL aggregate queries using SUM, COUNT, and AVG
SQL queries with hash joins between large tables
Python UDFs called from within a SQL query

questions.explanation: D is correct because Photon is a vectorized C++ execution engine that accelerates native SQL operations — Python UDFs break out of the Photon execution path because they run in the Python interpreter (via JVM), which Photon cannot accelerate. Queries that call Python UDFs fall back to the standard Spark execution engine for that operation. A is wrong because table scans with filter predicates are one of the primary operations Photon accelerates through vectorized I/O and predicate pushdown. B is wrong because aggregate functions like SUM, COUNT, and AVG are well-supported by Photon's vectorized aggregation. C is wrong because hash joins are explicitly supported by the Photon engine and see significant performance improvements.
questions.reference: Photon engine — supported and unsupported operations

questions.stem:
A data analyst creates a table in Databricks SQL without specifying a LOCATION clause:

```sql
CREATE TABLE analytics.sales_summary
USING DELTA
AS SELECT region, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue FROM raw.sales GROUP BY region;
```

Where will the data files for this table be stored?

options.text: In the analyst's personal home directory in DBFS
In the Databricks-managed storage location associated with the analytics schema (metastore-managed location)
In the same cloud storage path as the raw.sales source table
No data is stored because the table is a view over the raw.sales table

questions.explanation: B is correct because when a table is created without a LOCATION clause, it is a managed table and Databricks stores the data in the default storage location for the schema, which is managed by the metastore (Hive metastore or Unity Catalog). A is wrong because managed table data is stored in the schema's managed storage location, not in the analyst's personal directory. C is wrong because the new table creates independent data files from the CTAS query result — it is not a view and does not reference the source table's storage path. D is wrong because the CREATE TABLE ... AS SELECT statement creates a physical table with materialized data, not a view.
questions.reference: Managed tables — storage location

questions.stem:
A data analyst runs the following command:

```sql
DESCRIBE HISTORY sales.orders LIMIT 5;
```

What information will this command return?

options.text: The 5 most recent rows that were inserted into the orders table
The 5 most recent operations performed on the orders table, including version number, timestamp, and operation type
The 5 most recent SQL queries that selected from the orders table
The 5 most recent OPTIMIZE operations run on the orders table

questions.explanation: B is correct because DESCRIBE HISTORY is a Delta Lake command that returns the audit log of operations performed on a Delta table. Each row includes the version number, timestamp, user who performed the operation, the operation type (WRITE, MERGE, DELETE, OPTIMIZE, VACUUM, etc.), and operation parameters. The LIMIT 5 clause returns only the 5 most recent entries. A is wrong because DESCRIBE HISTORY shows table-level operations, not individual row inserts — row-level history requires change data feed. C is wrong because DESCRIBE HISTORY tracks write operations on the table, not SELECT queries run against it. D is wrong because it shows all operation types, not just OPTIMIZE — though OPTIMIZE entries would appear in the history if they were run.
questions.reference: DESCRIBE HISTORY

questions.stem: A data analyst runs `VACUUM sales.large_table RETAIN 0 HOURS` to immediately free up storage. What will happen?

options.text: The command succeeds and removes all files not referenced by the current table version
The command fails with an error because the default safety threshold is 168 hours (7 days) and 0 hours is below the minimum
The command removes only the transaction log entries, not the data files
The command succeeds but only removes files older than the table's creation date

questions.explanation: B is correct because Databricks enforces a minimum retention period of 168 hours (7 days) for VACUUM by default, and RETAIN 0 HOURS is below this threshold. Running VACUUM with a retention period shorter than 168 hours will raise an error unless the safety check is explicitly disabled using the configuration flag `spark.databricks.delta.retentionDurationCheck.enabled = false`. A is wrong because the safety check prevents VACUUM from running with retention below the 168-hour minimum. C is wrong because VACUUM removes unreferenced data files, not transaction log entries. D is wrong because the VACUUM retention period is based on the file modification timestamp relative to the current time, not the table creation date.
questions.reference: VACUUM retention and safety check

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to handle a column that may contain NULL values when calculating an average price. The analyst wants to substitute NULL values with 0 before averaging. Which function correctly replaces NULL with 0?

options.text: IFNULL(price, 0)
COALESCE(price, 0)
NVL(price, 0)
All of the above are valid in Spark SQL

questions.explanation: D is correct because all three functions — IFNULL, COALESCE, and NVL — are available in Spark SQL and all return the first non-NULL argument. COALESCE is the ANSI SQL standard and also accepts more than two arguments. NVL and IFNULL are convenience aliases that accept exactly two arguments. All three would correctly substitute NULL price values with 0 in this scenario. A, B, and C are each individually correct, but since all three work in Spark SQL, D is the most complete and accurate answer.
questions.reference: NULL handling — COALESCE, NVL, IFNULL

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to convert a string column `price_str` that contains values like '12.50', 'N/A', and '' (empty string) to a DOUBLE type. Some values are invalid and cannot be converted. Select TWO statements that are TRUE about this conversion scenario.

options.text: CAST(price_str AS DOUBLE) will return NULL for 'N/A' and '' without raising an error in Spark SQL
TRY_CAST(price_str AS DOUBLE) safely returns NULL for values that cannot be converted instead of raising an error
CAST(price_str AS DOUBLE) will raise a runtime error and abort the query when it encounters 'N/A'
TRY_CAST is only available for INTEGER conversions, not DOUBLE

questions.explanation: A is correct because in Spark SQL (unlike some other databases), CAST returns NULL for values that cannot be converted rather than raising a runtime error — 'N/A' and '' will silently become NULL values of type DOUBLE. B is correct because TRY_CAST is the explicit safe-cast function that guarantees NULL is returned for unconvertible values — it is more explicit about the intent of safe conversion and is available for DOUBLE and other types. C is wrong because Spark SQL's CAST is lenient by default and returns NULL rather than aborting the query on unconvertible values. D is wrong because TRY_CAST is available for all data types in Spark SQL, including DOUBLE, STRING, DATE, TIMESTAMP, and INTEGER.
questions.reference: CAST and TRY_CAST

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to return the first order date for each customer, ordered chronologically within each customer's order history. Which window function correctly returns the first value in an ordered window?

options.text: LEAD(order_date, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date)
LAG(order_date, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date)
FIRST_VALUE(order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date)
MIN(order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date)

questions.explanation: C is correct because FIRST_VALUE() returns the value from the first row in the window frame — when ordered by order_date ascending, this is the earliest order date for each customer, replicated across all rows in that customer's partition. A is wrong because LEAD returns the value from a row ahead of the current row (the next order), not the first row in the partition. B is wrong because LAG returns the value from a row behind the current row (the previous order), not the first row in the partition. D is wrong because while MIN() OVER a partition gives the minimum value (same logical result in this case), FIRST_VALUE() is the semantic function that correctly expresses 'the first value in the ordered window'.
questions.reference: Window functions — FIRST_VALUE

questions.stem:
A data analyst writes this query:

```sql
SELECT
  department,
  employee_name,
  salary,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS salary_rank
FROM employees
QUALIFY salary_rank = 1;
```

What does this query return?

options.text: All employees in each department ordered by salary descending
The highest-paid employee in each department (one row per department)
An error because QUALIFY is not valid Spark SQL syntax
All employees with a salary ranked number 1 across the entire company

questions.explanation: B is correct because ROW_NUMBER() assigns rank 1 to the first row in each department's partition when ordered by salary descending — the highest salary in each department. The QUALIFY clause filters the window function result to only rows where salary_rank = 1, returning exactly one row per department (the top earner). A is wrong because QUALIFY salary_rank = 1 filters to only rank 1 rows, not all rows. C is wrong because QUALIFY is valid Spark SQL syntax — it is the window function equivalent of HAVING for aggregate functions. D is wrong because ROW_NUMBER() is PARTITIONED BY department, so rank 1 is assigned within each department independently, not across the entire company.
questions.reference: Window functions — ROW_NUMBER with QUALIFY

questions.stem:
A data analyst writes this query to calculate a rolling 3-month average revenue:

```sql
SELECT
  month,
  revenue,
  AVG(revenue) OVER (
    ORDER BY month
    ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
  ) AS rolling_3mo_avg
FROM monthly_revenue;
```

The data has 6 months: Jan, Feb, Mar, Apr, May, Jun with revenues 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600. What will the `rolling_3mo_avg` value be for April?
questions.explanation: B is correct because for April (revenue = 400), the window 'ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW' includes the current row (April: 400) and the 2 rows before it (March: 300, February: 200). The average of these three values is (200 + 300 + 400) / 3 = 300. A is wrong because 400 is April's own revenue, not the 3-month average. C is wrong because 250 would be the average of only 2 months (Feb: 200, Mar: 300), not 3. D is wrong because 200 is February's revenue alone.
questions.reference: Window functions — ROWS BETWEEN frame specification

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to identify orders that exist in a `pending_orders` table but NOT in a `processed_orders` table, matching on `order_id`. Which JOIN type returns only rows from `pending_orders` that have no match in `processed_orders`?

options.text: INNER JOIN pending_orders p ON p.order_id = processed_orders.order_id
LEFT JOIN processed_orders po ON p.order_id = po.order_id WHERE po.order_id IS NULL
FULL OUTER JOIN processed_orders po ON p.order_id = po.order_id WHERE p.order_id IS NOT NULL
RIGHT JOIN processed_orders po ON p.order_id = po.order_id WHERE p.order_id IS NULL

questions.explanation: B is correct because a LEFT JOIN keeps all rows from the left table (pending_orders) and fills NULLs in the right table columns when there is no match. Adding WHERE po.order_id IS NULL filters to only those pending orders that had no match in processed_orders — effectively an anti-join using LEFT JOIN. A is wrong because an INNER JOIN returns only matching rows, which are the orders that exist in BOTH tables — the opposite of what is needed. C is wrong because the FULL OUTER JOIN condition WHERE p.order_id IS NOT NULL would return all orders that appear in pending_orders (including those that also appear in processed_orders), not just unmatched ones. D is wrong because a RIGHT JOIN keeps all processed_orders rows and the WHERE p.order_id IS NULL filter returns orders in processed_orders with no matching pending order — the exact opposite scenario.
questions.reference: JOIN types — LEFT JOIN anti-join pattern

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to combine first name and last name columns with a space between them, and also append the employee's department in parentheses. The columns are `first_name`, `last_name`, and `department`. Which expression produces 'John Smith (Engineering)'?

options.text: first_name + ' ' + last_name + ' (' + department + ')'
CONCAT(first_name, ' ', last_name, ' (', department, ')')
CONCAT_WS(' ', first_name, last_name, CONCAT('(', department, ')'))
Both B and C produce the correct result

questions.explanation: D is correct because both B and C produce 'John Smith (Engineering)'. CONCAT in option B directly concatenates all the pieces with explicit separators. CONCAT_WS in option C uses a space as the separator between the first three segments, and CONCAT('(', department, ')') correctly builds the parenthesized department. Both produce the same output. A is wrong because the + operator for string concatenation is not valid in Spark SQL — the correct operator-free approach uses CONCAT or ||, and + is used for numeric addition, which would raise a type error on strings.
questions.reference: String functions — CONCAT and CONCAT_WS

questions.stem:
A data analyst runs the following query:

```sql
SELECT
  customer_id,
  COUNT(*) AS order_count
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
ORDER BY order_count DESC;
```

A colleague suggests replacing `HAVING COUNT(*) > 5` with `WHERE COUNT(*) > 5`. What will happen if the colleague's change is made?

options.text: The query produces identical results because HAVING and WHERE are interchangeable
The query will produce an error because aggregate functions cannot be used in a WHERE clause
The query will filter rows before grouping, returning customers with more than 5 rows of any kind
The query will run slower because WHERE is evaluated after GROUP BY

questions.explanation: B is correct because WHERE clauses are evaluated before GROUP BY and cannot reference aggregate function results like COUNT(*). Placing an aggregate function in a WHERE clause is a SQL syntax error — Spark SQL will return an error indicating that aggregate functions are not allowed in WHERE clauses. A is wrong because HAVING and WHERE are not interchangeable — HAVING filters after aggregation, WHERE filters before. C is wrong because while WHERE does filter rows before grouping, using an aggregate function in WHERE is invalid SQL syntax and produces an error rather than filtering by row count. D is wrong because WHERE is evaluated before GROUP BY (making it faster for row filtering), but aggregate functions in WHERE cause errors regardless of performance.
questions.reference: HAVING vs WHERE with aggregates

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to count the number of distinct customers who placed orders in each month. The analyst considers several approaches. Select TWO queries that correctly count distinct customers per month.

options.text: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers FROM orders GROUP BY 1
SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, COUNT(customer_id) AS unique_customers FROM orders GROUP BY 1
WITH monthly AS (SELECT DISTINCT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, customer_id FROM orders) SELECT month, COUNT(*) AS unique_customers FROM monthly GROUP BY month
SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, SUM(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers FROM orders GROUP BY 1

questions.explanation: A is correct because COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) directly counts the number of unique customer IDs in each monthly group, which is exactly the requirement. C is correct because the CTE first deduplicates customer_id per month using DISTINCT, then counts the resulting deduplicated rows per month — this produces the same result as COUNT(DISTINCT). B is wrong because COUNT(customer_id) without DISTINCT counts all order rows per month including repeat orders from the same customer, not unique customers. D is wrong because SUM(DISTINCT customer_id) sums the unique customer ID numbers, not counts unique customers — for integer customer IDs this produces a meaningless sum, not a count.
questions.reference: COUNT DISTINCT and deduplication patterns

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to add 30 days to a date column `renewal_date` to calculate the grace period expiration. The `renewal_date` column is of type DATE. Which function correctly returns the date 30 days after `renewal_date`?

options.text: DATEADD(renewal_date, 30)
renewal_date + INTERVAL 30 DAYS
DATE_ADD(renewal_date, 30)
Both B and C return the correct result in Spark SQL

questions.explanation: D is correct because both DATE_ADD(date, n) and the INTERVAL syntax (date + INTERVAL 30 DAYS) are valid in Spark SQL and both return the date 30 days after the specified date. A is wrong because DATEADD is not a valid Spark SQL function — it is a SQL Server function. The correct Spark SQL function is DATE_ADD (not DATEADD). B is valid Spark SQL syntax using interval arithmetic. C is valid Spark SQL using the DATE_ADD function. Since both B and C work correctly, D is the most complete answer.
questions.reference: Date functions — DATE_ADD and INTERVAL

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to look up the category name for each product. Products without a category should still appear in the results with a NULL category name. The `products` table has `product_id` and `category_id`. The `categories` table has `category_id` and `category_name`. Some products have a `category_id` that does not exist in the `categories` table. Which JOIN returns all products, including those with unmatched or NULL category IDs?

options.text: INNER JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
LEFT JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
RIGHT JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
FULL OUTER JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id

questions.explanation: B is correct because a LEFT JOIN keeps all rows from the left table (products) regardless of whether a match exists in the right table (categories). Products with a category_id that doesn't exist in categories, or with a NULL category_id, will appear in the results with NULL values in the category_name column. A is wrong because INNER JOIN only returns products that have a matching category_id in the categories table, excluding products with unmatched or NULL category IDs. C is wrong because RIGHT JOIN keeps all categories rows (even those with no products) but may drop products with unmatched category IDs. D is wrong because FULL OUTER JOIN would include all products AND all categories (including categories with no products), adding more rows than required.
questions.reference: LEFT JOIN for preserving all left-table rows

questions.stem: A data analyst is asked to create a visualization showing the market share of 5 products as a percentage of total sales. The total must add up to 100%. Which chart type is most appropriate for this specific use case?

options.text: Line chart
Pie chart
Scatter plot
Bar chart

questions.explanation: B is correct because pie charts are designed specifically to show proportional composition of a whole — each slice represents a percentage of the total, making them appropriate when the parts sum to 100%. With only 5 products, the pie chart remains readable without being overly cluttered. A is wrong because line charts show change over time and are not suited for proportional composition at a single point in time. C is wrong because scatter plots show correlation between two continuous variables, not proportional breakdown. D is wrong because while a bar chart can show relative sizes, it does not inherently communicate 'parts of a whole that sum to 100%' as effectively as a pie chart for this specific use case.
questions.reference: Chart types — pie charts for proportions

questions.stem:
A data analyst is building a Databricks SQL dashboard that includes a query with the following parameter:

```sql
SELECT * FROM sales
WHERE order_date BETWEEN '{{ start_date }}' AND '{{ end_date }}'
```

What type of parameter widget should be added to the dashboard to allow users to select these date values?

options.text: Two text input parameters, one named start_date and one named end_date
A date parameter for each of start_date and end_date, which renders as a calendar date picker
A single date range parameter that automatically populates both start_date and end_date
A query-based dropdown parameter populated from a SELECT DISTINCT order_date query

questions.explanation: B is correct because the query uses two separate parameters (start_date and end_date), and each should be configured as a Date parameter type in Databricks SQL, which presents a calendar date picker to dashboard users for a better user experience than free text. A is wrong because while text parameters would technically work if users type valid date strings, they are error-prone and do not provide a date picker UI. C is wrong because Databricks SQL does not have a single built-in 'date range' parameter type that automatically populates two separate named parameters — start_date and end_date are distinct parameters that each need their own widget. D is wrong because a query-based dropdown selects from a list of discrete values and does not provide calendar-based date selection, which is more appropriate for arbitrary date ranges.
questions.reference: Parameterized queries — Date parameter type

questions.stem: A data analyst creates a dashboard showing weekly sales by product. The dashboard is shared with 50 regional managers, each of whom should only see data for their own region. The analyst cannot create 50 separate dashboards. Which approach correctly implements this requirement in Databricks SQL?

options.text: Use a region dropdown parameter in the dashboard query and share the dashboard with CAN_RUN permission so each manager selects their own region
Create a single dashboard with row-level security on the underlying Delta table using dynamic views that filter by the CURRENT_USER() function
Share a separate copy of each query for each manager with a hardcoded region filter in the WHERE clause
Use the CAN_VIEW permission, which automatically filters data based on the viewer's user group membership

questions.explanation: B is correct because dynamic views using CURRENT_USER() automatically filter the view output based on the identity of the user running the query. When a manager runs the dashboard, the underlying view returns only rows for their region without any manual parameter selection. This scales to any number of users without requiring per-user configurations. A is wrong because a dropdown parameter requires each manager to manually select their region and trusts them to only look at their own data — it does not enforce data isolation. C is wrong because creating 50 separate copies of queries is not maintainable and defeats the purpose of a shared dashboard. D is wrong because CAN_VIEW is a dashboard permission level, not a data-filtering mechanism — it does not filter data based on user group membership.
questions.reference: Dynamic views with CURRENT_USER() for row-level security

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to visualize the relationship between two continuous variables: advertising spend (X-axis) and website traffic (Y-axis) across 500 campaigns. The analyst wants to see whether higher ad spend is associated with higher traffic. Which chart type is most appropriate?

options.text: Line chart
Bar chart
Scatter plot
Counter widget

questions.explanation: C is correct because scatter plots are specifically designed to reveal correlations, clusters, and outliers between two continuous variables by plotting individual data points with one variable on each axis. With 500 campaigns as data points, the scatter plot will visually reveal whether there is a positive correlation between ad spend and traffic. A is wrong because line charts connect sequential data points and imply a temporal or ordered relationship between adjacent points — they are not appropriate for showing correlation between two independent variables. B is wrong because bar charts compare discrete categories on one axis against a numeric value, not the relationship between two continuous variables. D is wrong because a counter widget displays a single aggregate number, not the distribution of individual data points.
questions.reference: Chart types — scatter plots for correlation

questions.stem: A data analyst creates an alert in Databricks SQL with the condition: 'Value is ABOVE 1000'. The query currently returns 1500. The alert fires and sends a notification. The next scheduled run returns 900. What is the alert status after the second run, and what happens next?

options.text: The alert remains TRIGGERED because it was already triggered once and must be manually reset
The alert changes to OK status because 900 is below the threshold; a recovery notification may be sent if configured
The alert status becomes UNKNOWN because the value dropped unexpectedly
The alert changes to OK but sends another TRIGGERED notification for confirmation

questions.explanation: B is correct because Databricks SQL Alerts continuously evaluate the condition on each scheduled run. When the value drops to 900 (below the threshold of 1000), the alert transitions from TRIGGERED to OK status. If the alert is configured to send a notification when it recovers to OK, a recovery notification is sent. A is wrong because alerts do not require manual resets — they evaluate state automatically on each run. C is wrong because UNKNOWN is not a standard Databricks SQL Alert state — states are OK and TRIGGERED (plus a transient state when the query has not been run yet). D is wrong because the transition from TRIGGERED to OK does not send a new TRIGGERED notification — it sends an OK/recovery notification if that option is enabled.
questions.reference: Alert state transitions — TRIGGERED to OK

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to show managers a table of the top 20 customers by revenue on a dashboard, displaying customer name, segment, region, and total revenue. Which Databricks SQL visualization type should be used to display raw tabular data?

options.text: Counter widget
Table visualization
Heatmap
Bar chart

questions.explanation: B is correct because the Table visualization type in Databricks SQL displays query results in a structured, sortable row-and-column format, which is the appropriate way to show multi-column customer data with specific attribute values. A is wrong because a counter widget shows a single numeric value, not a multi-column dataset of 20 rows. C is wrong because a heatmap shows value intensity across two dimensional axes using color — it cannot display multi-column text/numeric rows as a table. D is wrong because a bar chart aggregates data into discrete bars for comparison — it cannot display the mix of text and numeric columns needed to show customer details.
questions.reference: Table visualization for raw data display

questions.stem: A data analyst publishes a Databricks SQL dashboard and wants to ensure it always shows fresh data. The underlying queries take about 2 minutes to run. Select TWO TRUE statements about dashboard refresh behavior.

options.text: A scheduled refresh will automatically re-run the underlying queries at the configured interval and update the dashboard cache
When a viewer opens the dashboard, it always triggers a fresh query execution regardless of the cache state
If a dashboard query ran 20 minutes ago and the result cache is still valid, viewers opening the dashboard will see the cached result without re-running the query
Dashboard scheduled refresh and the 24-hour query result cache operate independently — the scheduled refresh updates results even if the cache has not expired

questions.explanation: A is correct because a scheduled refresh is specifically designed to automatically re-run queries at a set interval (e.g., every hour, every day), updating the cached results so that viewers see current data without manually triggering a refresh. D is correct because the scheduled refresh and the query result cache are independent mechanisms — a scheduled refresh explicitly triggers query re-execution and updates results regardless of the 24-hour cache TTL state. B is wrong because opening a dashboard does NOT always trigger a fresh query execution — if valid cached results exist, viewers see those immediately without re-running the query (which is the purpose of caching). C is correct as a standalone statement, but since D is also correct and the question asks for TWO true statements, A and D are the primary answers that distinguish key dashboard refresh concepts.
questions.reference: Dashboard refresh and query result cache interaction

questions.stem: A data analyst is designing a dashboard to show sales performance by salesperson and product category. There are 12 salespeople and 8 product categories, resulting in 96 combinations. The analyst wants to see at a glance which salesperson-category combinations are performing above or below target. Which chart type best supports this comparison across 96 cells?

options.text: Bar chart with 12 groups of 8 bars each
Line chart with 12 separate lines
Heatmap with salespeople as rows and categories as columns
Pie chart with 96 slices

questions.explanation: C is correct because a heatmap is the optimal chart type for comparing a metric across two categorical dimensions simultaneously. With salespeople as rows (12) and product categories as columns (8), the heatmap creates a 12x8 grid where each cell's color immediately signals whether performance is above or below target. A is wrong because 12 groups of 8 bars creates a cluttered visualization that is difficult to compare across salespeople and categories simultaneously — pattern recognition across 96 bars is cognitively demanding. B is wrong because line charts imply a sequential or temporal relationship between adjacent points — the 8 product categories do not have a natural order, making a line chart misleading here. D is wrong because a pie chart with 96 slices would be unreadable — slices would be tiny and indistinguishable from each other.
questions.reference: Chart types — heatmap for 2D comparison

questions.stem:
A data analyst creates a Databricks SQL dashboard with a Text parameter named `search_term`. The parameter value is used in a SQL query:

```sql
SELECT * FROM products WHERE product_name LIKE '%{{ search_term }}%'
```

A user types `'%'; DROP TABLE products; --` into the text parameter. What protection does Databricks SQL provide in this scenario?

options.text: No protection — the SQL injection payload executes and drops the products table
Databricks SQL automatically escapes user input in parameters, preventing SQL injection — the literal text is used as a search string
The query fails with a syntax error but the table is not dropped
The parameter type 'Text' only accepts alphanumeric characters, blocking special characters

questions.explanation: B is correct because Databricks SQL parameterized queries using the `{{ parameter_name }}` syntax treat parameter values as literal string inputs, not as raw SQL fragments. The parameter value is safely substituted as a string literal, so the single quotes and SQL keywords in the injection attempt are treated as literal characters to search for, not as SQL syntax to execute. A is wrong because Databricks SQL's parameter substitution does not directly interpolate raw SQL — it uses parameterized query binding that prevents injection. C is wrong because the query does not fail — it safely searches for the literal string including the single quotes and SQL keywords. D is wrong because the Text parameter type does not restrict which characters can be entered — the protection comes from the parameterized execution model, not character filtering.
questions.reference: Parameterized queries — SQL injection protection

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to investigate what queries have run on a SQL Warehouse over the past week, specifically to find any queries that were cancelled or failed. Which Databricks SQL feature provides a log of warehouse-level events such as cluster start and stop events, along with query statuses?

options.text: Query History page — filter by status FAILED or CANCELLED
SQL Warehouse Event History tab — shows cluster lifecycle events
Databricks Unity Catalog lineage graph — shows query flows across tables
Query History page provides query statuses; the warehouse Event History tab provides cluster lifecycle events — both are needed together

questions.explanation: D is correct because these are two separate features in Databricks SQL. The Query History page lets you filter by status (including FAILED, CANCELLED, RUNNING, FINISHED) to find problematic queries. The warehouse's Event History tab in the warehouse detail view logs warehouse-level events such as cluster starts, stops, and scaling events. Together they provide the full picture of what happened to queries and the underlying compute. A is wrong because it describes only the Query History — warehouse lifecycle events are in the Event History. B is wrong because the Event History tab shows cluster lifecycle, not query-level statuses. C is wrong because Unity Catalog lineage tracks data lineage (which tables a query read/wrote), not query execution status.
questions.reference: Query History and SQL Warehouse Event History

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to explicitly cache a large reference table in a SQL Warehouse so that subsequent queries run against that cached version rather than reading from cloud storage each time. Which SQL statement forces a table's data into the disk cache?

options.text: CACHE TABLE catalog.schema.reference_table
CACHE SELECT * FROM catalog.schema.reference_table
ALTER TABLE catalog.schema.reference_table SET CACHED = TRUE
PERSIST TABLE catalog.schema.reference_table INTO MEMORY

questions.explanation: B is correct because `CACHE SELECT` is the Databricks SQL syntax for explicitly caching the result of a query into the disk (I/O) cache on the warehouse nodes. Running CACHE SELECT * FROM table forces all the table's data files to be read from remote storage and loaded into local SSD cache, so that subsequent scans of the same data are served from the fast local cache instead of cloud storage. A is wrong because CACHE TABLE is not the valid Databricks SQL syntax for explicit cache population; CACHE SELECT is the correct form. C is wrong because ALTER TABLE ... SET CACHED = TRUE is not a valid Databricks SQL statement for the disk cache. D is wrong because PERSIST TABLE is not valid Databricks SQL syntax.
questions.reference: CACHE SELECT — explicit disk cache population

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to label all queries submitted from a specific dashboard with a custom tag so that those queries are identifiable in Query History and can be tracked for cost attribution. Which Databricks SQL feature supports adding custom labels to queries?

options.text: Query comments — add /* dashboard: executive_kpis */ at the start of each SQL query
Query tagging — set a query tag using SET spark.databricks.workload.name or add tags via the query API
Dashboard metadata — add key-value labels in the dashboard settings page that auto-apply to all queries
Query History labels — manually tag queries in the Query History UI after they run

questions.explanation: B is correct because Databricks SQL supports query tagging through the `SET` command (e.g., `SET spark.databricks.workload.name = 'executive_kpis'`) or through the query API's tag parameter. Tagged queries appear with the tag in Query History and system tables, enabling cost attribution and workload monitoring. A is wrong because SQL comments are visible in the query text but are not indexed or filterable as structured tags in Query History. C is wrong because there is no dashboard-level metadata setting that auto-applies structured tags to all queries run from that dashboard. D is wrong because there is no manual labeling capability in the Query History UI after queries have run.
questions.reference: Query tagging for cost attribution and workload tracking

questions.stem: A data engineering team has a large Parquet dataset stored in cloud storage and wants to start using Delta Lake features (Time Travel, ACID transactions, schema enforcement) on this data without migrating it to a new location. Which SQL command converts existing Parquet files in-place to Delta format?

options.text: ALTER TABLE parquet.`/path/to/data` SET FORMAT DELTA
CONVERT TO DELTA parquet.`/path/to/data`
CREATE TABLE delta_table USING DELTA AS SELECT * FROM parquet.`/path/to/data`
UPGRADE TABLE parquet.`/path/to/data` TO DELTA

questions.explanation: B is correct because `CONVERT TO DELTA` is the Delta Lake command that converts an existing Parquet table to Delta format in place. It adds a Delta transaction log to the existing Parquet files without rewriting the data files, enabling Delta features immediately. A is wrong because ALTER TABLE ... SET FORMAT DELTA is not a valid Databricks SQL command for format conversion. C is wrong because CREATE TABLE ... AS SELECT creates a new Delta table by copying data — it does not convert the Parquet files in place. D is wrong because UPGRADE TABLE ... TO DELTA is not a valid Databricks SQL command; the correct command is CONVERT TO DELTA.
questions.reference: CONVERT TO DELTA — in-place Parquet to Delta conversion

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to view the custom key-value properties stored on a Delta table, including properties like 'owner', 'retention_days', and 'data_classification' that were set using ALTER TABLE ... SET TBLPROPERTIES. Which SQL command retrieves these properties?

options.text: DESCRIBE EXTENDED sales.orders
SHOW TBLPROPERTIES sales.orders
SELECT * FROM information_schema.table_properties WHERE table_name = 'orders'
DESCRIBE DETAIL sales.orders

questions.explanation: B is correct because `SHOW TBLPROPERTIES <table>` is the dedicated Databricks SQL command that returns all custom key-value properties stored on a Delta table as set by ALTER TABLE ... SET TBLPROPERTIES. It returns a clean key-value result set showing each property name and value. A is wrong because while DESCRIBE EXTENDED does include table properties in its output, it returns a large amount of other metadata and the properties are embedded within a mixed output — SHOW TBLPROPERTIES is the purpose-built command. C is wrong because querying information_schema.table_properties directly is not the standard Databricks SQL approach for reading tblproperties. D is wrong because DESCRIBE DETAIL shows storage-level metadata (file count, size, format, location) but does not show custom TBLPROPERTIES key-value pairs.
questions.reference: SHOW TBLPROPERTIES — viewing Delta table properties

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to optimize a Delta table's query performance for queries that heavily filter on `product_category` and `sale_date`. Select TWO SQL operations that together will maximize data skipping for these filter columns.

options.text: OPTIMIZE sales.transactions ZORDER BY (product_category, sale_date)
ANALYZE TABLE sales.transactions COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR COLUMNS product_category, sale_date
VACUUM sales.transactions RETAIN 0 HOURS
CREATE INDEX ON sales.transactions (product_category, sale_date)

questions.explanation: A is correct because OPTIMIZE with ZORDER BY co-locates related values of product_category and sale_date within the same data files, maximizing Delta Lake's file-level data skipping when queries filter on these columns. B is correct because ANALYZE TABLE COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR COLUMNS collects column-level statistics (min, max, null count, distinct count) that the query optimizer uses to make better decisions about which files to skip and how to order joins. Together, ZORDER ensures related data is co-located and ANALYZE ensures the optimizer has accurate statistics to exploit that co-location. C is wrong because VACUUM removes unreferenced files and has no effect on query optimization for the specified columns. D is wrong because Delta Lake does not support traditional B-tree indexes — file-level statistics and Z-Ordering are the Delta Lake mechanisms for data skipping.
questions.reference: OPTIMIZE ZORDER BY and ANALYZE TABLE for query optimization

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to pad a product code string on the left with zeros to ensure it is always exactly 8 characters long. For example, '1234' should become '00001234'. Which Spark SQL function accomplishes this?

options.text: RPAD(product_code, 8, '0')
LPAD(product_code, 8, '0')
ZFILL(product_code, 8)
FORMAT('%08d', product_code)

questions.explanation: B is correct because LPAD(string, length, pad_string) pads the string on the LEFT with the pad character until the total length reaches the specified value. LPAD('1234', 8, '0') returns '00001234'. A is wrong because RPAD pads on the RIGHT — RPAD('1234', 8, '0') would produce '12340000', not '00001234'. C is wrong because ZFILL is not a valid Spark SQL function. D is wrong because FORMAT with printf-style patterns is used for numeric formatting and the syntax shown is not standard Spark SQL for this purpose — LPAD is the correct choice.
questions.reference: String functions — LPAD for left padding

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to find the position of the first occurrence of the substring 'ERROR' within a log message string column `log_text`. If 'ERROR' is not found, the function should return 0. Which Spark SQL function returns the position of a substring within a string?

options.text: LOCATE('ERROR', log_text)
INSTR(log_text, 'ERROR')
CHARINDEX('ERROR', log_text)
Both A and B are valid Spark SQL functions that return the position

questions.explanation: D is correct because both LOCATE and INSTR are valid Spark SQL functions that return the 1-based position of a substring within a string, returning 0 if not found. LOCATE('ERROR', log_text) searches for 'ERROR' in log_text. INSTR(log_text, 'ERROR') does the same with the argument order reversed. Both return 0 when the substring is absent. A is correct on its own. B is correct on its own. Since both work correctly in Spark SQL, D is the most complete answer. C is wrong because CHARINDEX is a SQL Server function and is not supported in Spark SQL.
questions.reference: String functions — INSTR and LOCATE for substring position

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to format a revenue number as a string with thousands separators and 2 decimal places for display in a report (e.g., 1234567.89 should display as '1,234,567.89'). Which Spark SQL function produces this formatted number string?

options.text: TO_CHAR(revenue, '9,999,999.99')
FORMAT_NUMBER(revenue, 2)
ROUND(revenue, 2)
CAST(revenue AS DECIMAL(15,2))

questions.explanation: B is correct because FORMAT_NUMBER(number, decimal_places) is the Spark SQL function that formats a number as a string with locale-appropriate thousands separators (commas for en-US locale) and the specified number of decimal places. FORMAT_NUMBER(1234567.89, 2) returns '1,234,567.89'. A is wrong because TO_CHAR with Oracle-style format masks is not standard Spark SQL syntax. C is wrong because ROUND returns a numeric value (not a formatted string) and does not add thousands separators. D is wrong because CAST to DECIMAL changes the data type but does not produce a human-readable formatted string with separators.
questions.reference: FORMAT_NUMBER — numeric string formatting with separators

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to filter a result set to only include rows where a window function value meets a condition, without wrapping the entire query in a subquery. Which SQL clause filters on window function results directly within the same SELECT statement?

options.text: WHERE clause — add the window function condition to the existing WHERE clause
HAVING clause — aggregate the window function results and filter with HAVING
QUALIFY clause — filter on window function results after they are computed
Window functions cannot be filtered directly — a subquery or CTE is always required

questions.explanation: C is correct because the QUALIFY clause is specifically designed to filter rows based on window function results, similar to how HAVING filters aggregate results. QUALIFY is evaluated after the window functions are computed, allowing you to write conditions like QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) = 1 without needing a subquery. A is wrong because WHERE is evaluated before window functions are computed — window function references in WHERE are not valid and produce an error. B is wrong because HAVING operates on aggregate functions (GROUP BY results), not window function results — using window functions in HAVING is not valid. D is wrong because QUALIFY eliminates the need for a subquery when filtering window function results.
questions.reference: QUALIFY clause — filtering window function results

questions.stem:
A data analyst writes the following CASE expression:

```sql
SELECT
  order_id,
  CASE
    WHEN shipped_date IS NULL THEN 'Pending'
    WHEN shipped_date <= due_date THEN 'On Time'
    ELSE 'Late'
  END AS delivery_status
FROM orders;
```

An order has `shipped_date = NULL` and `due_date = '2024-06-01'`. What value does `delivery_status` return for this row?

options.text: 'On Time', because NULL is not greater than due_date
'Pending', because the first WHEN condition (IS NULL) is matched first
'Late', because NULL comparisons default to the ELSE branch
NULL, because a NULL shipped_date makes the entire CASE expression return NULL

questions.explanation: B is correct because a CASE expression evaluates its WHEN conditions sequentially and returns the result for the first condition that evaluates to TRUE. For this row, `shipped_date IS NULL` evaluates to TRUE immediately, so 'Pending' is returned and no further conditions are evaluated. A is wrong because the IS NULL check is the first condition and takes precedence — evaluation never reaches the `shipped_date <= due_date` comparison. C is wrong because the ELSE branch is only reached when no preceding WHEN condition is TRUE — here the first WHEN IS NULL is TRUE, so ELSE is never evaluated. D is wrong because the CASE expression returns a non-NULL string ('Pending') when the IS NULL check succeeds.
questions.reference: CASE expression with IS NULL — sequential evaluation

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to find the maximum revenue value among three columns: `online_revenue`, `store_revenue`, and `other_revenue` for each row. Some columns may be NULL. Select TWO TRUE statements about using GREATEST for this purpose in Spark SQL.

options.text: GREATEST(online_revenue, store_revenue, other_revenue) returns NULL if any of the three arguments is NULL
GREATEST(online_revenue, store_revenue, other_revenue) skips NULL arguments and returns the maximum of the non-NULL values
To safely ignore NULLs, wrap each argument in COALESCE: GREATEST(COALESCE(online_revenue, 0), COALESCE(store_revenue, 0), COALESCE(other_revenue, 0))
GREATEST with NULL arguments always returns 0 as a default

questions.explanation: A is correct because in Spark SQL, GREATEST (like most SQL functions) propagates NULLs — if any argument is NULL, the result is NULL. This is the default behavior. C is correct because the recommended workaround for NULL-safe GREATEST is to wrap each argument in COALESCE with a neutral value (0 for revenue), so that NULLs are replaced with 0 before GREATEST evaluates — ensuring a non-NULL result based on available values. B is wrong because Spark SQL's GREATEST does NOT skip NULLs — it returns NULL if any input is NULL (unlike some other databases that skip NULLs). D is wrong because GREATEST never returns 0 as a default for NULL inputs — it returns NULL.
questions.reference: GREATEST and LEAST — NULL propagation behavior

questions.stem: A data analyst creates a Databricks SQL dashboard and wants to apply custom number formatting to a revenue column in a Table visualization — specifically displaying values as currency with a dollar sign, comma separators, and no decimal places (e.g., '$1,234,567'). Where in Databricks SQL is this column formatting configured?

options.text: In the SQL query itself, using FORMAT_NUMBER() to format the value before it reaches the visualization
In the Table visualization settings, under the column configuration options where number format and display rules can be specified per column
In the dashboard's global styling settings, which apply a single format to all numeric columns across all visualizations
Number formatting in table columns is not supported in Databricks SQL — the raw number must be formatted in SQL

questions.explanation: B is correct because Databricks SQL Table visualizations include per-column configuration options where you can specify the display format for number columns — including currency symbols, decimal places, and thousands separators. These settings are applied at the visualization layer without modifying the underlying query. A is wrong because while SQL-level formatting (FORMAT_NUMBER, CONCAT with '$') is technically possible, it converts the value to a string and loses the ability to sort numerically — configuring formatting in the visualization settings is the preferred approach. C is wrong because there is no global dashboard styling setting that universally formats all numeric columns — formatting is configured per column per visualization. D is wrong because Databricks SQL Table visualizations do support number formatting configuration.
questions.reference: Table visualization — column number formatting

questions.stem: A data analyst is building a Databricks SQL dashboard with many complex queries and notices that the dashboard takes a long time to fully load. A colleague suggests consolidating multiple individual queries into fewer queries that use JOINs to retrieve all needed data. Why does this approach improve dashboard performance?

options.text: Fewer queries reduce the number of separate warehouse compute requests, decreasing total query scheduling overhead and network round-trips to the warehouse
Joined queries scan less total data than separate queries because Delta Lake applies cross-query predicate pushdown
The SQL Warehouse can only execute one query at a time, so reducing query count decreases queuing time
Joining queries in one statement allows Databricks to automatically cache all intermediate results

questions.explanation: A is correct because each individual query on a dashboard requires a separate round-trip to submit to the SQL Warehouse, be scheduled, executed, and have results returned. When a dashboard has 20 separate queries, there are 20 round-trips and 20 scheduling events. Consolidating into fewer JOIN-based queries reduces the number of warehouse interactions, scheduling overhead, and concurrent result fetches — improving overall dashboard load time. B is wrong because Delta Lake predicate pushdown operates within a single query, not across separate queries — separate queries do not share predicate context. C is wrong because SQL Warehouses can execute multiple queries concurrently (especially with auto-scaling), so the single-queue assumption is incorrect. D is wrong because intermediate results within a query are not automatically cached in a way that persists across future queries — caching applies to final query result sets.
questions.reference: Dashboard performance — reducing query count via joins

questions.stem: A data analyst publishes a Databricks SQL dashboard and needs to share it with stakeholders. Later, the analyst updates the dashboard layout and query logic significantly. A stakeholder asks to revert to the previous version of the dashboard. Which Databricks SQL capability supports this?

options.text: Delta Time Travel on the dashboard's underlying tables can restore the dashboard to a previous state
Databricks SQL dashboards maintain a version history that allows restoring or viewing previous versions of the dashboard
Dashboard versioning is not supported — the analyst must manually recreate the previous layout
The analyst can restore the dashboard by running RESTORE DASHBOARD sales_kpis TO VERSION AS OF 1

questions.explanation: B is correct because Databricks SQL dashboards (particularly AI/BI Dashboards) maintain a version history that tracks changes over time. Dashboard owners can view previous versions and restore to an earlier state, enabling recovery from unintended changes. A is wrong because Delta Time Travel applies to Delta table data, not to dashboard definitions — restoring a table does not restore a dashboard layout. C is wrong because dashboard version history is a supported feature in Databricks SQL. D is wrong because RESTORE DASHBOARD is not a valid SQL command — dashboard version management is done through the Databricks SQL UI, not through SQL syntax.
questions.reference: Dashboard version history and restoration
